message.content: Explain the tradeoffs between optimistic and pessimistic locking for our inventory service, and recommend one.

content.text:
Two flavors:

Pessimistic locking: take a row lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) at the start of a transaction. Other writers wait. Guarantees serialized access but costs latency under contention and can cause deadlocks if lock order is inconsistent.

Optimistic locking: read, compute, then UPDATE ... WHERE version = :v and re-read the affected row count. If zero, another writer won. You retry. No lock held across the decision window, so throughput scales with contention-free paths but you pay in retry cost when conflicts are common.

For inventory specifically: most SKUs see low concurrent writes (one stock adjustment at a time), but a few hot SKUs under flash-sale conditions get heavy concurrent decrements. Optimistic works well on the cold tail; on the hot head it degrades because retry rates spike.

Recommendation: optimistic locking as the default with a per-SKU concurrency tier — hot SKUs (identified by rolling write rate) escalate to pessimistic row locks. This keeps the common case cheap and bounds worst-case retries.

message.content: What metric do you use to promote a SKU into the hot tier?

content.text: A 60-second rolling count of update attempts per SKU. If it crosses, say, 20 writes/minute, the SKU is marked hot and its next transaction uses FOR UPDATE. Demotion after 5 minutes below the threshold. The numbers are starting points — you'd tune them from production traces.

message.content: And if a SKU is ambiguous — borderline hot?

content.text: Hysteresis handles it: separate promote (20/min) and demote (15/min) thresholds so a SKU flickering around 20 doesn't toggle lock strategies every minute. The gap between the two thresholds is the stability margin.
